items.title: Landing a Big Tech Internship as a UCLA student
items.author: Andrew Cruz
items.description:
Everything that matters for tech internship recruiting and what you should be doing as a UCLA CS student.

Internship recruiting is a rite of passage for every computer science student.

Each year brings the same familiar cycle: grinding through endless LeetCode problems, submitting hundreds of applications, and constantly refreshing your inbox in hopes of a reply.

As a rising third-year computer science major at UCLA, I’ve experienced my fair share of rejections and final-round disappointments. This summer, I’m incredibly lucky to be interning at Apple as a software engineer in Los Angeles 😄

The road here was far from smooth. In this post, I’ll break down how I approached recruiting, what I wish I’d done differently, and what I think actually makes a difference when it comes to landing an internship.

I’ll also share my experience studying computer science at UCLA, plus advice for future and incoming Bruins on how to make the most of their time here. Feel free to jump ahead!

What You Really Need to Know

For those of you who are just starting your internship search, here are some of the most important points that I wish I knew as an incoming freshman.

1. The first person to read your resume isn’t a person at all.

Most large companies receive so many applications that they rely on Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) and, more recently, AI to scan and rank resumes before a human ever sees them. If your resume lacks the right keywords, formatting, or even the right school, it might not be reviewed at all.

If your resume does make it past the software, the first human to read it will likely be a technical recruiter. However, despite “technical” being in the job title, these recruiters do not always have a technical background and may not fully understand the details of your projects or experience. In many cases, they are more familiar with industry buzzwords than technical concepts and will skim resumes quickly, often spending just 20 to 30 seconds scanning for well-known company names and standout bullet points.

There are a few key things that really matter on your resume. First, list the relevant classes you’ve taken. Recruiters will always want to see a Data Structures & Algorithms (DSA) course, along with any upper-division computer science classes you’ve taken. At UCLA, CS 32 can pass as a DSA class, but you’ll really want to take CS 180 to cover algorithms completely.
Second, highlight the programming languages and frameworks you used in your previous internships, research, or projects. On my own resume, I try to include a different technology on every line to cover as many bases as possible.
Beyond that, there are a ton of dos and don’ts. For example, don’t put any high school-related experiences on your resume, but do try to quantify your achievements when possible. In general, it’s hard to say what works and what doesn’t, but I would argue that these points are a good place to start.

Now, you might be thinking: “Why not just fill my resume with a bunch of buzzwords to catch their attention?” The problem is that the next person to read your resume will probably be an engineer with a strong technical background. They will be able to spot any exaggerated claims or buzzwords, and anything you include on your resume is fair game in an interview.

Writing your resume for three separate audiences can be challenging, and it’s crucial to understand this from the start.

2. If you don’t apply within the first 48 hours, you might already be too late.

There are a couple of reasons why applying early really matters. First, most companies review applications on a rolling basis. This means that the earlier you apply, the better your chances of being considered for the role.

Second, internship postings get flooded with applicants within hours. Most students look for roles in the same places: LinkedIn and a few shared GitHub repositories that track internship openings. These sources are updated daily, and thousands of students are monitoring them, so roles posted there can fill up fast.

There are a few popular Github repos for tracking internship openings.
1. Simplify CSC: https://github.com/SimplifyJobs/Summer2026-Internships
2. Vansh & Ouckah: https://github.com/vanshb03/Summer2026-Internships
3. SpeedApply: https://github.com/speedyapply/2026-SWE-College-Jobs
I’ve mainly used the Simplify CSC repo, which is the most popular and lists openings across various areas of tech.

Companies typically post internships starting as early as the summer before the role starts, with recruiting lasting into the following spring. To stay competitive, start early and check postings regularly.

From personal experience, I’ve seen postings close before I even had a chance to apply, and a few where I genuinely believe I would have gotten a first-round interview if I had just applied earlier. Realizing you missed the window can be one of the more frustrating parts of internship recruiting, so it’s very important to apply as soon as things drop.

3. Networking is valuable if you put in the effort.

You will often see people brag about landing an offer from a single cold message. While networking can be incredibly helpful, it won’t magically open doors for you unless you really put in the effort.

Networking tends to fall into two main categories: (1) career exploration and (2) finding job opportunities. The first is about talking to people, learning what they do, and understanding how they got there. The second is more goal-oriented and involves looking for referrals or potential openings.

Since coming to UCLA, I’ve had dozens of one-on-one calls with engineers, managers, and recruiters from various companies. These conversations have been incredibly helpful for learning about different career paths, company cultures, and what recruiters look for in candidates. If you’re just getting started with recruiting, setting up these kinds of chats can be a great way to ease into the process.

Many of these conversations have also helped me secure referrals and even interviews later on. Most large tech companies allow employee referrals, and the people you talk to will often be willing to help (keep in mind that referrals at larger companies hold less weight due to the volume of candidates). Cold messaging can be especially effective when reaching out to startups where the hiring process is more personal. I haven’t personally tried this approach, but I’ve heard numerous success stories from others who have.

Understanding how referrals work can be tricky because the process varies from company to company. One key point is that referrals tend to carry different levels of influence. For example, a referral from a manager usually carries more weight than one from a senior engineer, which in turn tends to be stronger than a referral from a junior engineer.
Getting a referral can take a lot of effort and might not even make a meaningful difference in many cases. A lot of students decide it’s more effective to apply in quantity, sometimes sending out 200 to 300 applications in a year, rather than trying to get a referral for every single one.

One challenge you might run into with networking is that not many people have the time or are interested in responding to cold messages. Out of the hundreds of cold messages I’ve sent on LinkedIn and email, only a few dozen replied, and even fewer were willing to chat. While this is completely understandable, it can make networking feel like a numbers game at times. Alternatively, I’ve had much more success reaching out to family friends, friends of friends, or even more distant connections to get introductions.

UCLA’s alumni network is decent. One of its biggest strengths is its size. You’ll find UCLA grads at nearly every tech company, and the larger ones probably have thousands. On the downside, many alumni don’t respond to cold outreach unless you have a personal connection. Another issue is that UCLA does a poor job of promoting its alumni network, considering I only found out about our official networking site (https://uclaone.com) just a few weeks ago.

One final point: there are plenty of networking “hacks” people use to improve their chances of landing an offer. One strategy I’ve seen is finding the recruiter assigned to your school and reaching out to them directly. Others have asked their referrers to personally vouch for them to the hiring manager, rather than just submitting a resume through the referral portal. It might take a bit of extra effort, but these kinds of approaches can help get your application in front of the right eyes and ultimately make a big difference.

4. You absolutely should be building side projects.

My number one piece of advice for students looking for their first internship is to build projects. If you don’t have a lot of experience on your resume, side projects are the best way to show off your skills and initiative.

Projects are valuable because they make great talking points in interviews and show that you can apply what you’ve learned outside of the classroom. Recruiters will often look for a dedicated projects section on your resume, so having solid examples is arguably even more important than your GPA or what classes you’ve taken.

Side projects have helped me get every one of my internships. From random Chrome extensions in high school to more polished web and mobile apps in college, these projects not only helped my resume stand out, but also gave me something concrete to talk about in interviews.
Interviewers often ask about teamwork and personal initiative. The last thing they want to hear about is your class group project. Having personal projects will give you more memorable stories to share and help you stand out both technically and inter-personally.

Building something on your own can feel intimidating at first, but anyone with some basic coding experience is capable of doing it. There are plenty of guides and starter ideas online to help you get going. If you need somewhere to start, I have even put together workshop curriculum on full-stack development that will give you all the skills you need to make your first project.

If building independent projects isn’t something you enjoy, college is the perfect time to join clubs or meet people who can help you work on group projects instead. At the end of the day, the most important thing is that you’re building projects and showing why a company should hire you.

5. Technical interview prep is non-negotiable.

Online Assessments, or OAs, are take-home technical assessments that companies use to evaluate an applicant’s coding abilities. Some companies send OAs to every applicant, others only send them to applicants that pass an initial resume screen, and some may skip them altogether.

For most companies, OAs will consist of a couple “LeetCode” style questions that have to be solved within a certain time constraint (usually around 1 hour). Unless you have experience with competitive programming, you will have to study a good amount (think 3+ months) to get a handle on the type of problems that might be thrown at you.

There are a couple of popular resources for studying LeetCode:
LeetCode 150: https://leetcode.com/studyplan/top-interview-150/
NeetCode: https://neetcode.io/practice
Beyond that, there are tons of YouTube videos and other resources on the internet that can be really helpful for studying.

Because internships are so competitive, companies often use the OA stage to quickly filter out candidates. Anything short of a perfect score can hurt your chances, so it’s important to be comfortable solving these kinds of problems under time pressure.

Beyond the OA, knowing how to solve LeetCode questions is crucial because you will often be asked the same type of questions in technical interviews. However, keep in mind that they’re evaluated very differently.

In an OA, your score is typically based on how many test cases your code passes. That means your solution needs to be fully correct and functional. In a technical interview, the focus is more on your thought process and how clearly you can explain your approach. In many cases, you’re not even expected to write fully working code.

Both OAs and technical interviews can be challenging in different ways, so it’s important to practice and build your confidence for each.

6. The school you attend makes a big difference.

The reality of searching for a software job in 2025 is that the school you attend will significantly impact your chances of being considered at every stage of the recruitment process. While each company approaches recruiting differently, many maintain a short list of schools they actively recruit from. One manager I talked to put it bluntly: “Stanford, CMU, Berkeley, UW, and GT resumes go on the top. UCLA resumes might end up in the middle. Anything below that won’t even be considered.”

This is clearly an extreme example. Plenty of students from non-target schools still land internships at Big Tech companies, but it’s a useful way to set your expectations going into the process. If you go to a non-target school, you’ll likely have to put more effort into applying, networking, and building your resume to stay competitive.

UCLA is ranked the #14 undergraduate computer science program by US News and is not a bad school for computer science by any means. While it may not stand out on a resume the way schools like CMU or Stanford do, UCLA is still strong enough that most tech companies actively consider students here. A quick LinkedIn search shows that UCLA has consistently been a solid pipeline to Big Tech. This year in particular, I would even guess that over 100 UCLA students interned at Amazon alone.
That said, there is a reason UCLA is a top public school without a top CS program. Our career center doesn’t have strong connections with many top tech companies, and UCLA lacks the on-campus recruiting presence that schools like Berkeley or Stanford benefit from. To be fair, I haven’t personally attended a career fair at UCLA, but I’ve consistently heard that they’re not particularly useful. Los Angeles is a also smaller tech hub with a less established startup culture, meaning that it can be more challenging to break into the startup scene.

7. Your graduation date can often be a deciding factor.

Companies prefer to hire third-year students because they’ve usually taken more classes, have more experience, and are just one year away from being eligible for full-time roles. Many companies will filter out first and second year students during the initial resume screen, so listing your actual graduation date as a freshman or sophomore can hurt your chances.

Even if you do make it to the interview stage, having a later grad date can still work against you. Many companies are strict about only hiring students who are on track to graduate within a year or two. In some cases, your grad date can even be the deciding factor between you and another equally qualified candidate.

Should you lie about your graduation date? If you’re a freshman, you probably shouldn’t list yourself as a junior unless you’re actually on track to graduate early. However, if you do have enough credits to finish in under four years, definitely put the earlier grad date on your resume. From what I’ve seen, it’s a pretty common strategy and can make a big difference in the recruitment process.

If you’ve tried recruiting as a first or second year student, you’ve probably heard of early career programs like Google STEP or Microsoft Explore. These internships are designed for freshmen and sophomores, and they’re often hyped as some of the best opportunities for younger students.

However, what often gets overlooked is that these programs primarily target students from historically underrepresented backgrounds in tech. It’s great that these programs exist, but if you don’t fall into one of those groups, your chances of being considered are close to zero. This is just something to keep in mind so you can set realistic expectations before applying.

8. Your GPA might not matter (that much).

You’ve probably heard the cliché: “C’s get degrees.” That might be true, but C’s probably won’t get you a good job.

3.5 is often tossed around as the benchmark GPA to stay competitive, but there’s really no magic number. Especially at top programs, CS classes can be tough and curves aren’t always generous. Most companies understand this and won’t automatically filter you out for a lower GPA. Of course, the closer to 4.0 the better, but a few points difference won’t make a meaningful difference (unless you want to pursue a more competitive field like quant for example).

Most UCLA computer science core courses are graded on a normal-esque curve. Depending on the professor, class averages usually fall in the B to B- range, and only about 10 to 20 percent of students earn an A. This is particularly true with many CS upper-div requirements and any class taught by Paul Eggert (CS35L, CS111, etc).
While that might seem like bad news for your GPA, it often evens out once you factor in general education and elective courses, which tend to grade more generously. A few B’s in core CS classes won’t tank your GPA, so being average or slightly above average in those classes is usually enough to stay competitive.

There are also a few good reasons not to obsess over a perfect GPA. First, the stress and workload required to get A’s in every class can be overwhelming. Even if you got straight A’s in high school, doing the same in college is a completely different challenge. Second, the extra time and energy it takes to chase perfect grades is often better spent elsewhere, including living your life and touching grass.

From what I’ve seen, recruiters care far more about what you’ve actually done than a few extra points on your GPA. Your past experience, the projects you’ve built, the skills you’ve developed, and to a lesser extent, the classes you’ve taken all carry more weight. When people ask me what to prioritize when trying to land their first internship, I always give the same advice: focus on building projects and learning new skills. Doing well in your classes is great, but it won’t mean much if you’re not doing anything outside the classroom.

9. There is a lot of luck involved.

Internship recruiting can be incredibly frustrating, especially when the process feels unclear and the results seem out of your control. There have been plenty of times where I felt like I did everything right, from the OA to the final interview, and still didn’t get the offer. And other times, I didn’t do anything special but somehow ended up with a lucky opportunity.

A company’s decision-making process for hiring interns is often unclear. Two frustrating practices that are helpful to know about are headcount and stack ranking.
Every company has a headcount cap on how many interns they can hire in a given year. Once headcount is reached, a company will stop hiring interns unless roles open up again. Some take this even further. For example, Amazon has a waitlist for candidates who passed the final interview but can’t receive an offer because headcount has already been filled.
Another frustrating practice used by some companies is called stack ranking. After each round of interviews, candidates are ranked not just on how they did in that specific interview, but also based on earlier performance and what’s on their resume (such as school, prior experience, etc.). So even if you “pass” an interview, you might not move on depending on how you compare to other candidates.

My best advice for handling the ups and downs of recruiting is to avoid dwelling on things that are out of your control and instead focus on the opportunities that are still out there. Chances are, you’ll be affected by headcount or stack ranking at some point. It can be incredibly frustrating, but it’s not something to take personally.

Making the Most of UCLA

I’ve gotten a lot of questions from UCLA computer science students about what they can do to get ahead. Most of my answers can be condensed into a few areas.

Major

In my opinion, your major doesn’t matter all that much if your goal is to land a software role. What really matters is the classes you take. As long as you take a few select CS classes (32, 35L, 111, 180), you should be equipped to recruit for software roles regardless of your major.

CS-adjacent majors (EE, CE, CSE, Math of Comp, Ling + CS, etc.) will have no problem taking these core CS courses and will even have the opportunity to take some of the same electives as CS majors. Engineering majors especially have a lot of flexibility through tech breadth and major electives to fit these courses in.

One common path at UCLA is starting as a Mathematics of Computation major and switching into CS later on. That’s the path I took, and I know a number of other students who’ve done the same without much trouble. If you’re planning to make this switch, I would keep in mind that UCLA is making it more difficult to switch into CS starting in 2025. The GPA requirement is increasing from 3.5 to 3.7, and non-CS majors will face stricter enrollment restrictions for CS classes. Overall, the process is becoming more competitive than it used to be.

One question you might have if you’re majoring in something other than CS is whether recruiters will perceive your application differently. From my experience, it’s not a major issue. Your major likely won’t have a significant impact on your chances, and many UCLA students tend to fudge their major a bit anyway (e.g., Math of Comp → Computer Science and Math, Ling and CS → Computer Science and Linguistics).

I know plenty of people who are sticking with a CS-adjacent major and still planning on going into software post-grad. As long as you’ve taken the right classes and have relevant experience, your major matters a lot less than you might think.

Classes

There are a few classes at UCLA that are definitely worth taking, either for the experience they provide or how they look on a resume:

CS 32: A solid introduction to C++ programming and data structures. Can also be a good opportunity to work on a larger software project (project 3)
CS 33: An essential course for embedded software roles, but otherwise not that important
CS 35L: Covers a wide range of software concepts (some more useful than others) and includes an extensive full-stack project that can definitely be put on a resume
CS 111: Goes over operating systems and how computers work, which are pretty important for software engineering
CS 180: A more in-depth algorithms course that is especially helpful for technical interview prep
CS M146, ECE C147, ECE 239AS, CS 163, and others: UCLA offers a handful of AI/ML related courses if that is an area you are interested in

I often get asked whether to take class X or class Y and what quarter is best for each class. The truth is that most of these questions need to be answered on a case-by-case basis. Oftentimes, the answer really depends on what classes you have already taken, the professor teaching the class, and what electives you plan on taking.

To get an idea of what classes you might take in your first two years, here’s every class I’ve taken so far:

Fall 23: Math 32A (multi pt.1), Physics 1A (mechanics), Sociology 1 (ge)
Winter 24: Math 33A (linear algebra), Physics 1B (electrostatics), CS 31 (intro to C++)
Spring 24: Math 32B (multi pt.2), Math 33B (differential equations), Physics 1C (magnetism), CS 32 (more C++)
Fall 24: Math 61 (discrete), Math 170E (probability), Physics 4AL (lab), CS33 (architecture)
Winter 25: ECE C147 (deep learning), Stats 10A (ge), Econ 1 (tech breadth), MUSCLG 68 (ge)
Spring 25: CS 35L (software), CS C121 (bio + machine learning), ECE 239AS (deep learning pt. 2), Econ 11 (science tech)

UCLA’s CS major is fairly long, given the number of lower-division math, physics, and CS classes required. Out of all the lower-div classes I’ve taken, I found the math classes to be the easiest by far. The physics classes varied a lot in difficulty depending on the professor, and the CS classes were manageable overall (with the exception of CS 35L, which is notoriously challenging).

One other thing to point out is that I only ended up taking one major-related class in Winter 25. That wasn’t intentional. I just couldn’t get the classes I wanted because of my enrollment time. At a large public university like UCLA, it is pretty common for classes to fill up before you get a chance to enroll.

Finally, if you’re an incoming freshman and want to get ahead, one way to prepare is by giving yourself a crash course on C++ (the first two CS courses you’ll take are both in C++). If you want more structure, you can probably find a CS 31 course website from a previous quarter with homework assignments and projects.

It can also be helpful to take a class or two at a community college before starting at UCLA. Many community colleges offer courses that can fulfill lower-div math and physics requirements ( it’s usually harder to transfer CS credits). Coming in with extra units can improve your enrollment time and may even help you graduate early.

Clubs

Getting involved in a professional-focused student organization early on is one of the most valuable things you can do as a new student at UCLA. In my experience, clubs have opened up a ton of opportunities I wouldn’t have found otherwise and also introduced me to a good chunk of my friends.

Unlike in high school, clubs in college can have competitive recruitment processes. My advice is to take club recruitment seriously as a freshman: show up to EAF (the club fair during Week 0), attend info sessions, apply broadly, put yourself out there as much as possible, and do research on each organization to boost your chances.

Here are some of the larger CS-related clubs at UCLA:

UCLA DevX: this is the first club that I joined as a freshman. DevX is a software-focused entrepreneurship club that brings together student developers, designers, and marketers to build unique software products. Being a part of DevX has allowed me work on some really cool projects (check out Retune) and introduced me to a ton of people doing all sorts of interesting things in tech.
LA Blueprint: similar to DevX except Blueprint works exclusively on contract-style projects with non-profits in LA. From what I have heard, Blueprint puts a lot of emphasis on mentoring new members and fostering a strong student community.
Nova: not as familiar with Nova but they sound pretty similar to Blueprint.
Creative Labs: similar to DevX in that they support student-led software projects. The main difference is that Creative Labs cycles new projects every quarter, so projects are often smaller in scope.
ACM: the largest CS club at UCLA. ACM is the only club on this list without an application to join and is composed of a bunch of committees focusing on various areas of tech (cloud, cyber, AI, etc.).
UPE: the official CS honor society at UCLA. UPE offers academic support for many CS classes and organizes professional development opportunities. UPE has a GPA threshold for joining but is known to be a strong organization for networking.

There are also several CS-adjacent clubs that involve a lot of software work and can offer strong project experience. I won’t go into as much detail about these, but they are worth checking out:

LA Hacks, UCLA Radio, Daily Bruin, Bruin Formula Racing, BruinSpace, Bruin AI, DataRes, and many more.

Clubs can be very competitive, and it’s easy to feel frustrated if things don’t go your way. I’ve been rejected from plenty of clubs myself, and it’s never fun knowing that another student decided what you can and can’t be part of. That said, UCLA has a huge range of student organizations, some of which are very open and not competitive at all, and you’ll eventually find your community if you put in the effort.

Final Thoughts

I’ve covered a lot in this article, but there’s still plenty I didn’t get into. At the end of the day, there’s no single roadmap for landing an internship, but I hope the points I shared here give you a solid starting point.

If you’re an incoming Bruin looking for a more detailed take on UCLA computer science, I also wrote this article last year that dives deeper into that (though my opinions of UCLA have definitely changed a bit since then).

If you’re just getting ready to apply for your first internship, good luck!

Understanding how referrals work can be tricky because the process varies from company to company. One key point is that referrals tend to carry different levels of influence. For example, a referral from a manager usually carries more weight than one from a senior engineer, which in turn tends to be stronger than a referral from a junior engineer.
Getting a referral can take a lot of effort and might not even make a meaningful difference in many cases. A lot of students decide it’s more effective to apply in quantity, sometimes sending out 200 to 300 applications in a year, rather than trying to get a referral for every single one.

One challenge you might run into with networking is that not many people have the time or are interested in responding to cold messages. Out of the hundreds of cold messages I’ve sent on LinkedIn and email, only a few dozen replied, and even fewer were willing to chat. While this is completely understandable, it can make networking feel like a numbers game at times. Alternatively, I’ve had much more success reaching out to family friends, friends of friends, or even more distant connections to get introductions.

UCLA’s alumni network is decent. One of its biggest strengths is its size. You’ll find UCLA grads at nearly every tech company, and the larger ones probably have thousands. On the downside, many alumni don’t respond to cold outreach unless you have a personal connection. Another issue is that UCLA does a poor job of promoting its alumni network, considering I only found out about our official networking site (https://uclaone.com) just a few weeks ago.

One final point: there are plenty of networking “hacks” people use to improve their chances of landing an offer. One strategy I’ve seen is finding the recruiter assigned to your school and reaching out to them directly. Others have asked their referrers to personally vouch for them to the hiring manager, rather than just submitting a resume through the referral portal. It might take a bit of extra effort, but these kinds of approaches can help get your application in front of the right eyes and ultimately make a big difference.

4. You absolutely should be building side projects.

My number one piece of advice for students looking for their first internship is to build projects. If you don’t have a lot of experience on your resume, side projects are the best way to show off your skills and initiative.

Projects are valuable because they make great talking points in interviews and show that you can apply what you’ve learned outside of the classroom. Recruiters will often look for a dedicated projects section on your resume, so having solid examples is arguably even more important than your GPA or what classes you’ve taken.

Side projects have helped me get every one of my internships. From random Chrome extensions in high school to more polished web and mobile apps in college, these projects not only helped my resume stand out, but also gave me something concrete to talk about in interviews.
Interviewers often ask about teamwork and personal initiative. The last thing they want to hear about is your class group project. Having personal projects will give you more memorable stories to share and help you stand out both technically and inter-personally.

Building something on your own can feel intimidating at first, but anyone with some basic coding experience is capable of doing it. There are plenty of guides and starter ideas online to help you get going. If you need somewhere to start, I have even put together workshop curriculum on full-stack development that will give you all the skills you need to make your first project.

If building independent projects isn’t something you enjoy, college is the perfect time to join clubs or meet people who can help you work on group projects instead. At the end of the day, the most important thing is that you’re building projects and showing why a company should hire you.

5. Technical interview prep is non-negotiable.

Online Assessments, or OAs, are take-home technical assessments that companies use to evaluate an applicant’s coding abilities. Some companies send OAs to every applicant, others only send them to applicants that pass an initial resume screen, and some may skip them altogether.

For most companies, OAs will consist of a couple “LeetCode” style questions that have to be solved within a certain time constraint (usually around 1 hour). Unless you have experience with competitive programming, you will have to study a good amount (think 3+ months) to get a handle on the type of problems that might be thrown at you.

There are a couple of popular resources for studying LeetCode:
LeetCode 150: https://leetcode.com/studyplan/top-interview-150/
NeetCode: https://neetcode.io/practice
Beyond that, there are tons of YouTube videos and other resources on the internet that can be really helpful for studying.

Because internships are so competitive, companies often use the OA stage to quickly filter out candidates. Anything short of a perfect score can hurt your chances, so it’s important to be comfortable solving these kinds of problems under time pressure.

Beyond the OA, knowing how to solve LeetCode questions is crucial because you will often be asked the same type of questions in technical interviews. However, keep in mind that they’re evaluated very differently.

In an OA, your score is typically based on how many test cases your code passes. That means your solution needs to be fully correct and functional. In a technical interview, the focus is more on your thought process and how clearly you can explain your approach. In many cases, you’re not even expected to write fully working code.

Both OAs and technical interviews can be challenging in different ways, so it’s important to practice and build your confidence for each.

6. The school you attend makes a big difference.

The reality of searching for a software job in 2025 is that the school you attend will significantly impact your chances of being considered at every stage of the recruitment process. While each company approaches recruiting differently, many maintain a short list of schools they actively recruit from. One manager I talked to put it bluntly: “Stanford, CMU, Berkeley, UW, and GT resumes go on the top. UCLA resumes might end up in the middle. Anything below that won’t even be considered.”

This is clearly an extreme example. Plenty of students from non-target schools still land internships at Big Tech companies, but it’s a useful way to set your expectations going into the process. If you go to a non-target school, you’ll likely have to put more effort into applying, networking, and building your resume to stay competitive.

UCLA is ranked the #14 undergraduate computer science program by US News and is not a bad school for computer science by any means. While it may not stand out on a resume the way schools like CMU or Stanford do, UCLA is still strong enough that most tech companies actively consider students here. A quick LinkedIn search shows that UCLA has consistently been a solid pipeline to Big Tech. This year in particular, I would even guess that over 100 UCLA students interned at Amazon alone.
That said, there is a reason UCLA is a top public school without a top CS program. Our career center doesn’t have strong connections with many top tech companies, and UCLA lacks the on-campus recruiting presence that schools like Berkeley or Stanford benefit from. To be fair, I haven’t personally attended a career fair at UCLA, but I’ve consistently heard that they’re not particularly useful. Los Angeles is a also smaller tech hub with a less established startup culture, meaning that it can be more challenging to break into the startup scene.

7. Your graduation date can often be a deciding factor.

Companies prefer to hire third-year students because they’ve usually taken more classes, have more experience, and are just one year away from being eligible for full-time roles. Many companies will filter out first and second year students during the initial resume screen, so listing your actual graduation date as a freshman or sophomore can hurt your chances.

Even if you do make it to the interview stage, having a later grad date can still work against you. Many companies are strict about only hiring students who are on track to graduate within a year or two. In some cases, your grad date can even be the deciding factor between you and another equally qualified candidate.

Should you lie about your graduation date? If you’re a freshman, you probably shouldn’t list yourself as a junior unless you’re actually on track to graduate early. However, if you do have enough credits to finish in under four years, definitely put the earlier grad date on your resume. From what I’ve seen, it’s a pretty common strategy and can make a big difference in the recruitment process.

If you’ve tried recruiting as a first or second year student, you’ve probably heard of early career programs like Google STEP or Microsoft Explore. These internships are designed for freshmen and sophomores, and they’re often hyped as some of the best opportunities for younger students.

However, what often gets overlooked is that these programs primarily target students from historically underrepresented backgrounds in tech. It’s great that these programs exist, but if you don’t fall into one of those groups, your chances of being considered are close to zero. This is just something to keep in mind so you can set realistic expectations before applying.

8. Your GPA might not matter (that much).

You’ve probably heard the cliché: “C’s get degrees.” That might be true, but C’s probably won’t get you a good job.

3.5 is often tossed around as the benchmark GPA to stay competitive, but there’s really no magic number. Especially at top programs, CS classes can be tough and curves aren’t always generous. Most companies understand this and won’t automatically filter you out for a lower GPA. Of course, the closer to 4.0 the better, but a few points difference won’t make a meaningful difference (unless you want to pursue a more competitive field like quant for example).

Most UCLA computer science core courses are graded on a normal-esque curve. Depending on the professor, class averages usually fall in the B to B- range, and only about 10 to 20 percent of students earn an A. This is particularly true with many CS upper-div requirements and any class taught by Paul Eggert (CS35L, CS111, etc).
While that might seem like bad news for your GPA, it often evens out once you factor in general education and elective courses, which tend to grade more generously. A few B’s in core CS classes won’t tank your GPA, so being average or slightly above average in those classes is usually enough to stay competitive.

There are also a few good reasons not to obsess over a perfect GPA. First, the stress and workload required to get A’s in every class can be overwhelming. Even if you got straight A’s in high school, doing the same in college is a completely different challenge. Second, the extra time and energy it takes to chase perfect grades is often better spent elsewhere, including living your life and touching grass.

From what I’ve seen, recruiters care far more about what you’ve actually done than a few extra points on your GPA. Your past experience, the projects you’ve built, the skills you’ve developed, and to a lesser extent, the classes you’ve taken all carry more weight. When people ask me what to prioritize when trying to land their first internship, I always give the same advice: focus on building projects and learning new skills. Doing well in your classes is great, but it won’t mean much if you’re not doing anything outside the classroom.

9. There is a lot of luck involved.

Internship recruiting can be incredibly frustrating, especially when the process feels unclear and the results seem out of your control. There have been plenty of times where I felt like I did everything right, from the OA to the final interview, and still didn’t get the offer. And other times, I didn’t do anything special but somehow ended up with a lucky opportunity.

A company’s decision-making process for hiring interns is often unclear. Two frustrating practices that are helpful to know about are headcount and stack ranking.
Every company has a headcount cap on how many interns they can hire in a given year. Once headcount is reached, a company will stop hiring interns unless roles open up again. Some take this even further. For example, Amazon has a waitlist for candidates who passed the final interview but can’t receive an offer because headcount has already been filled.
Another frustrating practice used by some companies is called stack ranking. After each round of interviews, candidates are ranked not just on how they did in that specific interview, but also based on earlier performance and what’s on their resume (such as school, prior experience, etc.). So even if you “pass” an interview, you might not move on depending on how you compare to other candidates.

My best advice for handling the ups and downs of recruiting is to avoid dwelling on things that are out of your control and instead focus on the opportunities that are still out there. Chances are, you’ll be affected by headcount or stack ranking at some point. It can be incredibly frustrating, but it’s not something to take personally.

Making the Most of UCLA

I’ve gotten a lot of questions from UCLA computer science students about what they can do to get ahead. Most of my answers can be condensed into a few areas.

Major

In my opinion, your major doesn’t matter all that much if your goal is to land a software role. What really matters is the classes you take. As long as you take a few select CS classes (32, 35L, 111, 180), you should be equipped to recruit for software roles regardless of your major.

CS-adjacent majors (EE, CE, CSE, Math of Comp, Ling + CS, etc.) will have no problem taking these core CS courses and will even have the opportunity to take some of the same electives as CS majors. Engineering majors especially have a lot of flexibility through tech breadth and major electives to fit these courses in.

One common path at UCLA is starting as a Mathematics of Computation major and switching into CS later on. That’s the path I took, and I know a number of other students who’ve done the same without much trouble. If you’re planning to make this switch, I would keep in mind that UCLA is making it more difficult to switch into CS starting in 2025. The GPA requirement is increasing from 3.5 to 3.7, and non-CS majors will face stricter enrollment restrictions for CS classes. Overall, the process is becoming more competitive than it used to be.

One question you might have if you’re majoring in something other than CS is whether recruiters will perceive your application differently. From my experience, it’s not a major issue. Your major likely won’t have a significant impact on your chances, and many UCLA students tend to fudge their major a bit anyway (e.g., Math of Comp → Computer Science and Math, Ling and CS → Computer Science and Linguistics).

I know plenty of people who are sticking with a CS-adjacent major and still planning on going into software post-grad. As long as you’ve taken the right classes and have relevant experience, your major matters a lot less than you might think.

Classes

There are a few classes at UCLA that are definitely worth taking, either for the experience they provide or how they look on a resume:

CS 32: A solid introduction to C++ programming and data structures. Can also be a good opportunity to work on a larger software project (project 3)
CS 33: An essential course for embedded software roles, but otherwise not that important
CS 35L: Covers a wide range of software concepts (some more useful than others) and includes an extensive full-stack project that can definitely be put on a resume
CS 111: Goes over operating systems and how computers work, which are pretty important for software engineering
CS 180: A more in-depth algorithms course that is especially helpful for technical interview prep
CS M146, ECE C147, ECE 239AS, CS 163, and others: UCLA offers a handful of AI/ML related courses if that is an area you are interested in

I often get asked whether to take class X or class Y and what quarter is best for each class. The truth is that most of these questions need to be answered on a case-by-case basis. Oftentimes, the answer really depends on what classes you have already taken, the professor teaching the class, and what electives you plan on taking.

To get an idea of what classes you might take in your first two years, here’s every class I’ve taken so far:

Fall 23: Math 32A (multi pt.1), Physics 1A (mechanics), Sociology 1 (ge)
Winter 24: Math 33A (linear algebra), Physics 1B (electrostatics), CS 31 (intro to C++)
Spring 24: Math 32B (multi pt.2), Math 33B (differential equations), Physics 1C (magnetism), CS 32 (more C++)
Fall 24: Math 61 (discrete), Math 170E (probability), Physics 4AL (lab), CS33 (architecture)
Winter 25: ECE C147 (deep learning), Stats 10A (ge), Econ 1 (tech breadth), MUSCLG 68 (ge)
Spring 25: CS 35L (software), CS C121 (bio + machine learning), ECE 239AS (deep learning pt. 2), Econ 11 (science tech)

UCLA’s CS major is fairly long, given the number of lower-division math, physics, and CS classes required. Out of all the lower-div classes I’ve taken, I found the math classes to be the easiest by far. The physics classes varied a lot in difficulty depending on the professor, and the CS classes were manageable overall (with the exception of CS 35L, which is notoriously challenging).

One other thing to point out is that I only ended up taking one major-related class in Winter 25. That wasn’t intentional. I just couldn’t get the classes I wanted because of my enrollment time. At a large public university like UCLA, it is pretty common for classes to fill up before you get a chance to enroll.

Finally, if you’re an incoming freshman and want to get ahead, one way to prepare is by giving yourself a crash course on C++ (the first two CS courses you’ll take are both in C++). If you want more structure, you can probably find a CS 31 course website from a previous quarter with homework assignments and projects.

It can also be helpful to take a class or two at a community college before starting at UCLA. Many community colleges offer courses that can fulfill lower-div math and physics requirements ( it’s usually harder to transfer CS credits). Coming in with extra units can improve your enrollment time and may even help you graduate early.

Clubs

Getting involved in a professional-focused student organization early on is one of the most valuable things you can do as a new student at UCLA. In my experience, clubs have opened up a ton of opportunities I wouldn’t have found otherwise and also introduced me to a good chunk of my friends.

Unlike in high school, clubs in college can have competitive recruitment processes. My advice is to take club recruitment seriously as a freshman: show up to EAF (the club fair during Week 0), attend info sessions, apply broadly, put yourself out there as much as possible, and do research on each organization to boost your chances.

Here are some of the larger CS-related clubs at UCLA:

UCLA DevX: this is the first club that I joined as a freshman. DevX is a software-focused entrepreneurship club that brings together student developers, designers, and marketers to build unique software products. Being a part of DevX has allowed me work on some really cool projects (check out Retune) and introduced me to a ton of people doing all sorts of interesting things in tech.
LA Blueprint: similar to DevX except Blueprint works exclusively on contract-style projects with non-profits in LA. From what I have heard, Blueprint puts a lot of emphasis on mentoring new members and fostering a strong student community.
Nova: not as familiar with Nova but they sound pretty similar to Blueprint.
Creative Labs: similar to DevX in that they support student-led software projects. The main difference is that Creative Labs cycles new projects every quarter, so projects are often smaller in scope.
ACM: the largest CS club at UCLA. ACM is the only club on this list without an application to join and is composed of a bunch of committees focusing on various areas of tech (cloud, cyber, AI, etc.).
UPE: the official CS honor society at UCLA. UPE offers academic support for many CS classes and organizes professional development opportunities. UPE has a GPA threshold for joining but is known to be a strong organization for networking.

There are also several CS-adjacent clubs that involve a lot of software work and can offer strong project experience. I won’t go into as much detail about these, but they are worth checking out:

LA Hacks, UCLA Radio, Daily Bruin, Bruin Formula Racing, BruinSpace, Bruin AI, DataRes, and many more.

Clubs can be very competitive, and it’s easy to feel frustrated if things don’t go your way. I’ve been rejected from plenty of clubs myself, and it’s never fun knowing that another student decided what you can and can’t be part of. That said, UCLA has a huge range of student organizations, some of which are very open and not competitive at all, and you’ll eventually find your community if you put in the effort.

Final Thoughts

I’ve covered a lot in this article, but there’s still plenty I didn’t get into. At the end of the day, there’s no single roadmap for landing an internship, but I hope the points I shared here give you a solid starting point.

If you’re an incoming Bruin looking for a more detailed take on UCLA computer science, I also wrote this article last year that dives deeper into that (though my opinions of UCLA have definitely changed a bit since then).

If you’re just getting ready to apply for your first internship, good luck!

items.title: UCLA Computer Science: Reflections from a First-Year Student
items.author: Andrew Cruz
items.description:
As my freshman year at UCLA concludes, I’m excited to share my initial thoughts on being a student here, specifically what it’s like to study computer science. This article delves into my experiences with coursework, extracurricular activities, and the engineering culture at UCLA.

I really enjoyed my first year at UCLA, but there are a few things I wish I had known and prepared for before starting school here. In this article, I offer some tips and observations, such as strategies for course registration and club involvement, to help future Bruins navigate their own UCLA journey.

Note: My current major is “Mathematics of Computation,” a combined math and computer science major in the College of Letters & Science. This major has nearly identical lower-division requirements to the computer science major in the School of Engineering, and I plan to switch to that major soon.

Classes

One of the main differences between UCLA computer science and computer science programs at other large public universities is its emphasis on breadth. During my freshman year, I took a mix of math, physics, and computer science classes. Here’s what my schedule looked like:

Fall: Math 32A (multi pt.1), Physics 1A (mechanics), Sociology 1
Winter: Math 33A (linear algebra), Physics 1B (electrostatics), CS 31 (intro to C++)
Spring: Math 32B (multi pt.2), Math 33B (differential equations), Physics 1C (magnetism), CS 32 (more C++)

Most engineering students take three or four core classes per quarter, sometimes adding a lab or general education requirement. All of the classes I’ve taken, except for Sociology 1, are lower-division requirements for the computer science major. Looking back at my first year, I would say that the most memorable academic experience was taking the Physics 1 series.

Physics 1 Series

All UCLA computer science majors are required to take the Physics 1 series, which is notorious for being a GPA crusher. This requirement is quite unusual, as most computer science programs at other universities typically only require one physics class. At UCLA, however, computer science students must complete three physics classes in addition to a lab.

Personally, I found Physics 1A and Physics 1B to be my most challenging courses this past year. Despite taking AP Physics C: Mechanics in high school, I still struggled with the higher level of difficulty in Physics 1A. Later, I found out that you can actually petition the engineering department to bypass Physics 1A with a score of 4 or 5 on the AP Mechanics exam.

Physics 1B proved to be even more challenging. Not only was it completely new material (I hate circuits), but our professor was new, gave out weekly quizzes, and made the exams extremely complicated. Luckily, the class was single-handedly saved by our TA (god bless Rayner).

As a first-year student, it’s important to recognize that many lower-division requirements, such as the Physics 1 series, are “weeder” classes known for their rigor and grade deflation. However, the difficulty of these classes can also vary based on the professor and the class cycle.

Professors

Early on, I discovered how a good professor can transform a challenging class into a bearable (and sometimes even enjoyable) experience. Like many schools, UCLA has its own professor rating website: Bruinwalk. When I found a Math 33B professor known to have a 70% A rate, I rushed to sign up for their class section. Conversely, when I realized the only professor teaching Math 61 had a sub-2 star rating, I opted to take that course a different quarter.

Cycle

Taking classes “on-cycle” versus “off-cycle” can also make a huge difference. Most computer science freshmen choose to take CS 31 starting in fall quarter, CS 32 in winter, and CS 33 in spring — this is considered “on-cycle”. With this in mind, I decided to delay taking these classes by one quarter, not enrolling in CS 31 until winter quarter. Although enrolling “off-cycle” put me a quarter behind other students, it proved beneficial. I encountered more manageable professors and a less rigorous grading curve. While my “on-cycle” friends complained constantly about the workload in their computer science classes, I found CS 31 and CS 32 to be notably less challenging. I probably watched a grand total of three lectures across both classes, though I still spent 30+ hours on the infamous CS 32 project 3.

Course Selection

Navigating course registration at UCLA can be daunting. I remember my first time trying to enroll in classes, only to find out that most of my desired courses were already full. Just like other large public universities, UCLA has the problem of having way too many students for the number of spots offered in each class. This means in-demand classes can be very difficult to get, especially for younger students with later enrollment times. Here’s some of the strategies I’ve learned to manage this process better:

1. Identify Backup Classes: Having plenty of backup classes is a must before course registration. Going into my enrollment time, I try to have eight or more different classes in mind with the hope of just getting three or four of them. For example, when Math 61 filled up last quarter, I had Math 32B and Math 33B as backups, saving me from a scheduling nightmare.
2. Be Intentional with First Pass and Second Pass: If you aren’t familiar with the two-pass system, first pass is the enrollment times for your first 10 units (two classes). Second pass comes after all first pass times are done and lets you sign up for your remaining classes. Therefore, prioritizing in-demand classes during first pass will often increase your likelihood of getting them.
3. Utilize PTE and ECR: If all else fails, many students have found success with PTE (Petition to Enroll) and ECR (Enrollment Consideration Request), which are last-ditch methods for getting classes that are already full. These methods are generally unreliable and are dependent on the professor/department.

Computer Science Curriculum

Beyond what I have already covered, one thing that stood out about UCLA academics is its computer science curriculum. UCLA’s introductory computer science courses are taught in C++, and I would bet that the curriculum hasn’t changed in the last 20 years or so (also evidenced by the class website http://web.cs.ucla.edu/classes/spring24/cs32/syllabus.html this thing looks like it was made in the stone age).

Even though learning an aging programming language is not a problem in itself, I would argue that UCLA’s computer science introductory courses are outdated and too conceptual. Whereas my friends at other universities are learning industry-applicable skills and technologies, I am stuck learning how to implement a doubly linked list with a dummy head node. Which is to say not very useful or interesting at all. I know that UCLA’s classes become more practical down the line, but these are some of my observations so far.

Clubs

Outside of coursework, extracurricular activities were an important part of my first year at UCLA. Unlike high school clubs, which are often used as gimmicks for college applications, academic clubs at the university-level are a lot more serious and can be great resume boosters.

UCLA has a ton of computer science-focused clubs, from national organizations like ACM and IEEE to smaller groups like Creative Labs and LA Hacks. Two clubs that I am actively involved in are DevX and DataRes Consulting.

DevX is a project-focused club where we work in 8–12 member teams to develop full-stack apps. I am currently working on an app called Retune, which is trying to improve music exploration by combining location sharing with Spotify activity. Next year, I will be on the DevX board as developer lead, running our club’s dev intern program.

DataRes Consulting is also a project-based club, but we work with startups to provide data science and machine learning consulting services. In spring quarter, I worked on a team developing an AI chatbot for the startup Chelle AI.

Being involved in academic clubs has been a great way to explore new technical areas and get more project experience, which is essential for any computer science major. It has also provided opportunities to meet new people, especially students from different grades. With my involvement in social clubs as well, participating in extracurricular activities has become a core and fun part of my UCLA experience.

It’s better to be overly involved in clubs initially — you can always cut back later. Use your first few quarters to explore different clubs while balancing academics and social life. UCLA offers a wide range of clubs, so it can be challenging to keep up. As an incoming student, attend info sessions for clubs that interest you and make sure to visit the Enormous Activities Fair during Week 0. Good luck!

Some Issues

One thing that has always puzzled me is the competitiveness of many clubs. For example, DevX’s sub-5% acceptance rate meant that even highly qualified students face rejection. This level of competition often feels more intense than applying to the university itself. To make things worse, many of these clubs choose to brag about their low acceptance rates and rigorous selection processes.

From what I have seen, academic clubs often face one of two problems.

1. Club A has great professional opportunities but is super elitist and difficult to get into.

2. Club B is open to all students but is disorganized and doesn’t end up doing much.

ACM is an example of Club B at UCLA. ACM positions itself as a beginner-friendly club catering to a variety of technical interests, with different committees for competitive programming, app development, cloud computing, and more. In theory, this sounds great. In practice, this broad structure can lead to disorganization and resource constraints. For instance, some of my friends in the ACM AI committee were assigned to projects that never got off the ground, while others didn’t get assigned to a project at all.

That said, some of my friends had excellent experiences in other ACM committees, like ACM Cyber. While the broad structure of ACM can create hurdles, there are areas within the club where members can find valuable opportunities.

What does this mean? It highlights the importance of being proactive and making the most of the resources available. While navigating clubs at UCLA and other universities can be challenging, actively engaging with them can can lead to meaningful experiences and personal growth.

Engineering Culture

Engineering at UCLA, like many large public universities, is heavily male-dominated, especially in computer science. However, one thing that sets UCLA apart is its embrace of engineers with non-traditional backgrounds.

While I have met many students like myself who have long known they wanted to study engineering, I have also encountered quite a few who hadn’t decided on engineering until committing to UCLA. For example, a friend of mine spent most of high school focusing on pre-health extracurriculars. After being accepted to UCLA for electrical engineering, they pivoted to EE despite having no prior experience in the field.

This means the range of students studying engineering at UCLA is quite diverse. In each of my classes, for every top student — the super-genius curve breakers that I would struggle to keep up with even if I tried — there are two students who are just beginning to navigate this level of coursework. While students at UCLA are still heavily academically inclined, the culture here is not as competitive or rigorous as I expected

Note: this is likely influenced by my background coming from a competitive Bay Area high school.

Work-Life Balance

One of the reasons I chose UCLA over other universities was its promise of a fun college experience, and it has not disappointed. I’ve been pleasantly surprised to find that even among engineering students, maintaining a healthy work-life balance is highly valued here.

UCLA is home to a huge athletic culture, where playing sports and hitting the gym is common — even amongst nerds. People love to go out and eat. The beach is only a few miles away. As a tennis player, living in LA has been a particular treat. With the second-largest tennis tournament in the U.S. just a couple of hours away, I’ve had the opportunity to watch top tennis pros practice right on our UCLA courts (as well as trek out to the tournament itself). With so many things nearby, living in LA is a lot of fun, making it one of the major perks of attending UCLA.

With so many things nearby, living in LA is a lot of fun, making it one of the major perks of attending UCLA.

Some Problems

While I appreciate a healthy work-life balance, I’ve noticed that career-focused motivation is frequently underemphasized within the engineering community. Since arriving at UCLA, I haven’t experienced a significant push to further my professional ambitions, and much of the career-oriented work I’ve done has been self-initiated.

What do I mean by career-oriented work? For computer science majors, this can be developing personal projects, practicing leetcode problems, networking with connections, and filling out hundreds of internship applications.

Career Resources

UCLA’s career resources do not provide an environment conducive to advancing careers in engineering and technology. The career center offers minimal help, and anyone who has attended a UCLA career fair knows the frustration of waiting in long lines without even getting a chance to speak one-on-one with a recruiter. From what I’ve heard, the career center does a poor job of fostering connections with tech companies, making UCLA even less of a recruiting hotspot for engineering.

To make matters worse, UCLA is not a target school for computer science. While our program is relatively highly ranked (top 15), it places us behind top-tier programs like Stanford, Berkeley, and the University of Washington. As a result, UCLA students lose a competitive edge when it comes to tech recruitment.

In my experience, UCLA’s alumni network is also limited in the support it provides. Although I’ve managed to arrange some coffee chats and secure a few referrals from former Bruins, the majority of alumni don’t seem interested in offering assistance. Compared to a school like USC, which is renowned for its strong alumni network, UCLA’s network is not as competitive.

Not to say that it is impossible for computer science students at UCLA to be successful. A quick scroll on LinkedIn reveals plenty of UCLA students with FAANG+ internships. There are still incredibly motivated individuals at UCLA, but the overall environment doesn’t seem to foster a high level of career-focused ambition.

Research Opportunities

If there is one thing UCLA does well, it is making research opportunities accessible to self-motivated students. Every one of my friends who tried to join a research lab managed to secure a research position by cold emailing professors (unfortunately, the official research portal is unreliable). Often, this research ends up being tangential to their career goals, such as doing bioinformatics as a data science major.

Final Thoughts

While career-focused students may be disappointed by UCLA’s lack of resources, self-motivated students will still find plenty of opportunities here, whether that be through clubs, research, or local startups. UCLA might not be as competitive or demanding as a school like Berkeley, but its emphasis on work-life balance can help students excel in other areas.

Transferring to Computer Science

Quick little tidbit about switching majors: Transferring to computer science is common and doable, which is great news for students like me who didn’t initially apply to the School of Engineering. All you need is a 3.5 GPA, an engineering workload (taking a science class and a math class in the same quarter), and completion of CS 31, 32, and 33.

Conclusion

As I reflect on my freshman year, I’m grateful for the opportunities and challenges that have shaped my journey so far. Attending college has been a huge wake-up call in terms of time and priority management, and UCLA has definitely helped me become more independent. While I still take great care to excel in my academics, I also make sure to stay involved with both academic and social clubs. Despite the demands of my part-time internship at Lockheed, I make it a point to take every opportunity to go out and enjoy myself. I continue to prioritize side projects and learning new technical skills while also emphasizing the importance of staying physically active.

This year, I’ve made tons of friends and gotten to play way too much poker, leaving me with very little to complain about. While I have some qualms with the career resources here, I believe UCLA offers a fantastic environment for self-motivated students to thrive. The diverse experiences and challenges at UCLA have made my freshman year incredibly memorable. I’m very excited to return in the fall and see what the next three years will bring.
